scenes.dialogue: Hi there! I need access to patient data to prepare for rounds tomorrow morning. Can you help me get into the EHR system?

choices.text: Sure, I'll give you full access to the EHR right now.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. This violates the Minimum Necessary Rule. Access should be limited to only what's needed for the specific task.

choices.text: Let's confirm if it's needed for treatment before accessing any records.
choices.feedback: Correct! HIPAA allows sharing PHI for treatment purposes, but it's important to verify the scope of access needed and ensure it's relevant to your duties.

choices.text: You can look up anything you need — it's all internal staff.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Even internal access must follow HIPAA rules. Access must be limited to job role and the Minimum Necessary standard.

choices.text: Check with your supervisor about the scope of your role first.
choices.feedback: Partial credit. While verifying role scope is good practice, HIPAA does permit treatment-related access. The key is ensuring it's limited to what's necessary.

scenes.dialogue: A patient's family member just called asking about their loved one's condition. They sound very worried. What should I tell them?

choices.text: Give them a full update — they're family and deserve to know.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. HIPAA requires patient authorization before disclosing PHI to family members, unless the patient has agreed or it's an emergency situation.

choices.text: Confirm the patient has authorized you to speak with this family member first.
choices.feedback: Correct! Before sharing any PHI with family members, you must verify that the patient has authorized this disclosure or that an exception applies.

choices.text: Tell them general information only, nothing specific.
choices.feedback: Partial credit. Even general information can be PHI. You still need patient authorization or a valid exception before any disclosure.

choices.text: Hang up immediately — you can't talk to anyone about patients.
choices.feedback: Not quite. While protecting PHI is important, HIPAA does allow disclosure to family members involved in care with proper authorization. Don't be rude — verify authorization instead.

scenes.dialogue: A colleague from another department wants to review patient charts for a quality improvement study. How should I handle this request?

choices.text: Share the charts — quality improvement benefits everyone.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Quality improvement activities require proper authorization and must follow the Minimum Necessary Rule. Verify the request is legitimate first.

choices.text: Verify they have proper authorization and need only the minimum necessary PHI.
choices.feedback: Correct! Quality improvement is a permitted use under HIPAA, but it must follow proper procedures including authorization and the Minimum Necessary Rule.

choices.text: Refuse — they're not on your team.
choices.feedback: Not quite. HIPAA does permit sharing PHI for healthcare operations like quality improvement, but proper authorization and minimum necessary standards must be followed.

choices.text: Give them access after they sign a confidentiality agreement.
choices.feedback: Partial credit. A confidentiality agreement is good, but you also need to verify proper authorization and ensure only minimum necessary information is shared.

scenes.dialogue: A patient has requested to see their medical records. What's the correct procedure?

choices.text: Tell them they need their doctor's permission first.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Under HIPAA's Privacy Rule, patients have the right to access their own medical records without needing permission from their healthcare provider.

choices.text: Provide access within 30 days, with possible 30-day extension if needed.
choices.feedback: Correct! HIPAA requires covered entities to provide access to medical records within 30 days of the request, with one possible 30-day extension.

choices.text: Give them the records immediately on the spot.
choices.feedback: Partial credit. While providing quick access is great service, HIPAA allows up to 30 days to fulfill the request. Immediate access isn't always required, though it's encouraged when possible.

choices.text: Explain that medical records are confidential and cannot be shared.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Patients have a fundamental right under HIPAA to access their own medical records. This is one of the core individual rights protected by the Privacy Rule.

scenes.dialogue: Thanks for helping me understand HIPAA better! One last scenario: A researcher wants patient data for a study. What should I do?

choices.text: Share de-identified data that removes all identifying information.
choices.feedback: Good approach! De-identified data is not considered PHI under HIPAA and can be shared more freely. However, verify the de-identification meets HIPAA standards.

choices.text: Require patient authorization or an IRB waiver before sharing any identifiable data.
choices.feedback: Correct! Research use of PHI requires either patient authorization or an Institutional Review Board (IRB) waiver under the Privacy Rule. Well done!

choices.text: Share the data — research is important for medical progress.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Research is valuable, but HIPAA requires proper authorization or an IRB waiver before sharing identifiable patient data for research purposes.

choices.text: Deny all research requests to be safe.
choices.feedback: Too restrictive. HIPAA does allow PHI use for research with proper authorization or IRB waiver. Blanket denials aren't necessary when proper procedures are followed.

scenes.dialogue: I'm on the phone with a patient's employer who's asking about their appointment time. They need to verify the employee was here. Should I confirm?

choices.text: Yes, confirming appointment times is fine — it's just scheduling info.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Even appointment dates/times are PHI under HIPAA. You need patient authorization before confirming to an employer.

choices.text: Ask if the patient authorized this disclosure first.
choices.feedback: Correct! Appointment information is PHI. Always verify patient authorization before confirming any information, even to an employer.

choices.text: Hang up immediately — never talk to employers.
choices.feedback: Not quite. While protecting PHI is important, HIPAA allows disclosure to employers with proper patient authorization. Be professional and verify authorization.

scenes.dialogue: You notice the sign-in sheet at reception shows the names of all patients who checked in today, plus their appointment times and reasons for visit. This is visible to anyone walking by.

choices.text: This is a HIPAA violation — patient names and visit reasons are PHI.
choices.feedback: Correct! Sign-in sheets that display other patients' information violate HIPAA. Best practice is to use methods that don't reveal others' PHI.

choices.text: This is fine — it's just a sign-in sheet.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Visible sign-in sheets that show other patients' names, times, and reasons for visit are a common HIPAA violation.

scenes.dialogue: You hear Riley discussing a patient's test results over the phone at the front desk, speaking loudly enough that others in the waiting room can hear specific medical details.

choices.text: This violates HIPAA — medical details shouldn't be discussed where others can hear.
choices.feedback: Correct! The Minimum Necessary rule requires limiting PHI disclosures. Discussions should occur in private areas, not where other patients can overhear.

choices.text: This is acceptable — it's a hospital, people expect to hear medical talk.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Even in medical settings, HIPAA requires reasonable safeguards to prevent incidental disclosures. Loud discussions of PHI in public areas violate this principle.

scenes.dialogue: The file cabinet containing patient medical records is unlocked and unattended. Anyone walking by could access these files. The room door is also propped open.

choices.text: This is a HIPAA violation — physical safeguards require securing PHI.
choices.feedback: Correct! HIPAA's Security Rule requires physical safeguards for PHI. Unlocked cabinets and open doors create unauthorized access risks.

choices.text: This is fine — it's in a records room, not a public area.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Location doesn't matter — PHI must be secured with locks and access controls to prevent unauthorized access, even in designated record areas.

scenes.dialogue: You find patient documents in the regular trash can instead of the designated shredder bin. The documents contain names, diagnoses, and Social Security numbers.

choices.text: This is a serious HIPAA violation — PHI must be properly destroyed.
choices.feedback: Correct! HIPAA requires proper disposal of PHI through shredding, burning, or other destruction methods. Regular trash disposal risks unauthorized access.

choices.text: This is fine — the trash is inside the building.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Disposing of PHI in regular trash is a serious violation. Janitors, cleaning crews, and others could access these documents. Always use secure disposal methods.

scenes.dialogue: I'm discussing a patient's case with a colleague in the hallway. We're keeping our voices down, so it should be okay, right?

choices.text: No — hallway conversations risk incidental disclosure. Use private areas.
choices.feedback: Correct! Even quiet conversations in hallways violate HIPAA's reasonable safeguards requirement. Use private offices, conference rooms, or designated areas for PHI discussions.

choices.text: Yes, as long as you're quiet, hallway discussions are fine.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Volume doesn't eliminate the risk. HIPAA requires reasonable efforts to prevent incidental disclosures, which means avoiding public areas for PHI discussions.

choices.text: It's fine — you're both doctors treating the same patient.
choices.feedback: Partial truth. While discussing patient care between authorized providers is allowed, HIPAA still requires reasonable safeguards against incidental disclosure. Find a private location.

scenes.dialogue: The ER status board shows patient names, room numbers, and chief complaints ("chest pain," "broken arm"). It's visible to anyone walking through the ER.

choices.text: This violates HIPAA — patient names linked to conditions shouldn't be publicly visible.
choices.feedback: Correct! While status boards serve operational needs, displaying names with medical information violates HIPAA. Use room numbers or codes instead of names.

choices.text: This is necessary for ER operations and therefore allowed.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. While status boards are useful, HIPAA doesn't provide an exception for convenience. Use alternative methods like coded systems that don't display patient names publicly.

scenes.dialogue: A computer workstation is left unlocked with a patient's full medical record displayed on screen. The staff member stepped away 10 minutes ago and hasn't returned.

choices.text: This is a HIPAA violation — workstations must be locked when unattended.
choices.feedback: Correct! HIPAA's Security Rule requires automatic logoff or manual lockout when leaving workstations unattended. This prevents unauthorized access to electronic PHI.

choices.text: This is fine — it's in a staff-only area.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Even in restricted areas, unattended workstations displaying PHI violate HIPAA. All staff members, not just authorized users, could access this information.

scenes.dialogue: Hey, did you hear about the celebrity who came into the ER last night? I saw their chart. Want to know what they were here for?

choices.text: No, and you shouldn't access or discuss patient records out of curiosity.
choices.feedback: Correct! Accessing PHI without a job-related reason violates HIPAA, even for staff members. Discussing it compounds the violation. Report this to a supervisor.

choices.text: Sure, tell me! We're both staff, so it's okay.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Being staff doesn't authorize curiosity-driven access to PHI. This is a serious HIPAA violation that can result in termination and legal penalties.

choices.text: I won't listen, but you do you.
choices.feedback: Insufficient. While not listening is good, you should actively discourage this behavior and report it. Allowing HIPAA violations to continue is a compliance failure.

scenes.dialogue: I need to access patient records to test a new system update. I'll only look at the technical data, not the medical information. Is this okay?

choices.text: Use test/dummy data instead of real patient records for system testing.
choices.feedback: Correct! IT personnel should use de-identified or dummy data for testing. Accessing real PHI requires specific authorization and a legitimate job-related need.

choices.text: Yes, as IT staff you have access to everything for system maintenance.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. IT staff must follow the Minimum Necessary rule and only access PHI when required for specific job duties. Testing should use dummy data.

choices.text: Access is fine as long as you don't share what you see.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Even viewing PHI without a legitimate need violates HIPAA. The fact that you don't share it doesn't eliminate the violation.

scenes.dialogue: You find a sticky note on a desk with a login username and password written on it. The credentials provide access to the patient database system.

choices.text: This is a HIPAA Security Rule violation — passwords must not be shared or written down.
choices.feedback: Correct! Written passwords violate HIPAA's Security Rule. Each user must have unique credentials that aren't shared, and passwords must be stored securely.

choices.text: This is fine — it's just for convenience and the desk is in a secure area.
choices.feedback: Incorrect. Written passwords create security risks regardless of location. HIPAA requires safeguarding authentication credentials. This must be reported and corrected.

scenes.dialogue: OMG, did you hear about the patient in Room 405? Apparently he came in with a—

choices.text: Let her finish, then say 'Yeah I heard about that too!'
choices.feedback: Your silence just made you an accessory to a HIPAA violation. The cafeteria has ears, and one of them might be that patient's lawyer. Intervene early, intervene politely, but INTERVENE.

choices.text: Interrupt: 'Stop. We can't discuss patients by room number in public areas.'
choices.feedback: Correct! Room numbers + public spaces = HIPAA violation combo. Minimum Necessary doesn't apply when the necessary amount is ZERO because you're in a cafeteria.

choices.text: Say nothing and just eat your sad desk salad faster
choices.feedback: Your silence enables bad behavior. The cafeteria has ears, and one of them might be that patient's lawyer. Intervene early, intervene politely, but INTERVENE.

choices.text: Change the subject: 'Hey, did you see that email about parking?'
choices.feedback: Passive avoidance doesn't address the violation. You need to actively shut down inappropriate PHI discussions in public spaces.

scenes.dialogue: Hi, I'm John Smith's husband. He was admitted yesterday and I'm at work—can you just tell me if his test results came back? I can't get him on the phone and I'm worried sick.

choices.text: He's stable, tests are pending. I'll have him call you when he wakes up.
choices.feedback: You just violated privacy by assuming authorization. That patient might be in the hospital BECAUSE of this 'husband.' Domestic violence, estranged relationships, and legal separations exist. Always verify before sharing.

choices.text: I cannot confirm or deny any patient information without authorization.
choices.feedback: Technically correct but needlessly robotic. You CAN share if the person is on the authorized contact list. Check first, then share if authorized.

choices.text: Let me verify you're on his authorized contact list first.
choices.feedback: Correct! HIPAA allows disclosure to 'persons involved in care' BUT you need to verify the relationship and that the patient hasn't restricted this person. Always verify before sharing. Two minutes of checking could prevent a lifetime of trauma.

choices.text: Pull up the chart to 'just check' if husband is listed as emergency contact
choices.feedback: You're already accessing the chart before verification. Verify identity and relationship FIRST, then access the authorized contacts. Don't put the cart before the horse.

scenes.dialogue: Oh no. Oh no no no. You just faxed discharge paperwork with full medical history, medications, and diagnosis to 555-0123 instead of 555-0132. That's... that's Dr. Peterson's rival practice. They're going to see EVERYTHING.

choices.text: Call the wrong number immediately, explain the error, request they destroy the fax
choices.feedback: Good first step, but not enough. This is a BREACH. You need to document everything and notify your Privacy Officer immediately. They'll conduct a risk assessment and determine if patient notification is required.

choices.text: Don't tell anyone and hope they just throw it away
choices.feedback: Congrats, you just turned a small mistake into a reportable breach with potential fines. The cover-up is always worse than the crime. Career suicide move.

choices.text: Tell your supervisor but wait to see if the other office calls you first
choices.feedback: Delays make it worse. This is a breach that requires immediate action: document, notify Privacy Officer, attempt retrieval, conduct risk assessment, and possibly notify the patient.

choices.text: Document the incident and notify your Privacy Officer immediately
choices.feedback: Correct! This is a BREACH. Unsecured PHI went to an unauthorized recipient. Your Privacy Officer needs to: (1) Document everything, (2) Attempt to retrieve/destroy the information, (3) Conduct risk assessment, (4) Possibly notify the patient. HIPAA has a whole process for this—use it.

scenes.dialogue: Hey, Jessica called in sick again. Third time this month. Can you pull her records and tell me what's actually going on? I need to know if this is legit or if she's just hungover.

choices.text: Sure, let me pull her chart... (He's HR, he's allowed to know, right?)
choices.feedback: You just violated a coworker's privacy to appease HR. Jessica's medical information is HERS, not the company's. Employers get work status, not diagnoses. Now Jessica can sue both you AND Brad. Congrats!

choices.text: Jessica can provide a fitness-for-duty note, but I can't share her medical information.
choices.feedback: Correct! Employers are NOT entitled to employee medical information under HIPAA. They can require a doctor's note confirming the employee can/can't work, but diagnosis and treatment details are off-limits. This is a trap. Brad has zero right to Jessica's chart, even if he signs your paycheck.

choices.text: Let me check with Jessica first and see if she'll authorize me to share.
choices.feedback: Better than just handing over the chart, but you're still treating this as negotiable. Employers don't get medical info, period. Jessica can provide a work note, but her diagnosis is none of HR's business.

choices.text: I can tell you dates of service but not diagnosis or treatment details.
choices.feedback: Still wrong. Even dates of service are PHI. HR gets a fitness-for-duty note from the employee, nothing from you. Don't share anything without authorization.

scenes.dialogue: Code VIP! We have a celebrity in the ER—reality TV star, you'd know the name. Press is already circling. I need you to: (1) Pull the chart and brief our PR team on the situation (2) Make sure all staff know to keep it quiet (3) Move the patient to a private wing ASAP. This is huge for the hospital's reputation. Handle it.

choices.text: Brief PR team with medical details, Alert staff to maintain confidentiality, Coordinate private wing transfer
choices.feedback: WRONG. You just violated HIPAA for PR purposes. Celebrity status doesn't override privacy rights. Sharing medical details with PR is a fireable offense and a lawsuit waiting to happen. The hospital's 'reputation' isn't worth the fines.

choices.text: Verify patient wants privacy protections, Alert staff to maintain confidentiality, Coordinate private wing transfer (if patient agrees)
choices.feedback: Correct! Even celebrities have HIPAA rights. You: (1) Verify the patient's wishes about privacy and accommodations, (2) Remind staff that celebrity status doesn't change HIPAA rules, (3) Offer enhanced privacy options if the patient wants them. The patient decides, not the administrator.

choices.text: Alert staff to maintain confidentiality, Coordinate private wing transfer, Prepare generic statement for PR (no medical details)
choices.feedback: Good effort, but you're assuming the patient wants special treatment without asking. Always verify patient preferences first. They might prefer standard care. Let the patient drive these decisions.

choices.text: Refuse all special accommodations to treat them like any other patient
choices.feedback: Too rigid. HIPAA allows offering enhanced privacy measures if the patient wants them. The key is patient choice, not blanket refusal. Ask what they prefer, then accommodate reasonable requests.

scenes.dialogue: I've been watching your performance. Now for the REAL test. Federal audit starts Monday, and I need to know you understand HIPAA inside and out. SCENARIO 1: You discover a colleague has been accessing patient charts they shouldn't have access to—including their ex-spouse's records. They beg you not to report it because they'll lose their job. What's your move?

choices.text: Report immediately to compliance and your supervisor.
choices.feedback: Correct! This is a serious HIPAA violation (improper access + potential revenge snooping). You're required to report it. Their job loss is a consequence of THEIR actions, not yours. Document what you saw and escalate immediately.

choices.text: Give them a warning but don't report this time.
choices.feedback: Wrong. By not reporting, YOU'RE now complicit in the violation. This isn't a judgment call—it's a mandatory reporting situation. Unauthorized access to medical records, especially for personal reasons, is a federal crime.

choices.text: Tell them to self-report and give them 24 hours to do it.
choices.feedback: Wrong. This isn't their decision to make (or delay). You have a duty to report HIPAA violations immediately. Waiting 24 hours could mean more violations, and you'll be held accountable for the delay.

choices.text: Mind your own business—it's not your problem.
choices.feedback: Disastrous. If you witness a HIPAA violation and don't report it, you can be personally fined and face criminal charges. This is exactly your problem. Report it immediately or face the consequences.

scenes.dialogue: SCENARIO 2: A patient's attorney calls demanding copies of medical records. They say it's for a malpractice case against another hospital, and they'll subpoena if you don't comply immediately. They're very aggressive and threatening. How do you respond?

choices.text: Verify the request is legitimate, confirm patient authorization, follow proper disclosure procedures.
choices.feedback: Correct! Don't be bullied by aggressive attorneys. You need: (1) Written authorization from the patient, OR (2) A valid court order/subpoena (which you must verify), (3) Follow minimum necessary standards, (4) Document everything. Threats don't bypass HIPAA.

choices.text: Comply immediately to avoid the subpoena.
choices.feedback: Wrong. Aggressive attorneys don't scare HIPAA into submission. You still need proper authorization or a valid court order. Handing over records because someone sounds scary is a violation. Always verify and follow procedure.

choices.text: Refuse to provide anything without a court order.
choices.feedback: Too rigid. Patient authorization is sufficient—you don't always need a court order. But you're right to push back on aggressive demands. Verify authorization, confirm the request is legitimate, then proceed appropriately.

choices.text: Transfer the call to your supervisor without gathering any information.
choices.feedback: Not terrible (escalation is smart), but you should still gather basic info first: who's calling, what they're requesting, and what authorization they claim to have. Then escalate with that context.

scenes.dialogue: Final question. SCENARIO 3: You're working late and notice the CEO of your hospital is logged into a workstation, looking at patient records. You're pretty sure they don't have a treatment relationship with these patients. The CEO outranks everyone. What do you do?

choices.text: Report the suspicious access to compliance, regardless of their title.
choices.feedback: Absolutely correct! HIPAA doesn't care about org charts. The CEO has no special rights to snoop in patient records. This is potentially a massive violation. Document what you saw, report to compliance, and let them investigate. Your job is to protect patient privacy, not the CEO's reputation.

choices.text: Politely ask the CEO if they need the access for a legitimate reason.
choices.feedback: Wrong. You're not their supervisor, and this isn't a casual conversation. Asking them directly could be seen as confrontational OR give them time to cover their tracks. Report to compliance and let them handle the investigation. Don't tip anyone off.

choices.text: Ignore it—they're the CEO, they probably have authorization.
choices.feedback: Catastrophic. The CEO isn't above HIPAA. If they're improperly accessing records, you have a duty to report it. 'They're important' is not a defense. Ignoring this makes you complicit in what could be a massive violation.

choices.text: Mention it to your supervisor the next day but don't make it urgent.
choices.feedback: Too slow. HIPAA violations need immediate reporting, regardless of who's involved. Waiting until 'next day' could mean more violations and shows you don't understand the urgency. Report immediately to compliance.

scenes.dialogue: I'm investigating a domestic assault. The victim is in your ER right now. I need to see their medical records immediately—this is an active investigation and time is critical.

choices.text: Of course, officer. Let me pull those records for you right away.
choices.feedback: Wrong. Police badge doesn't bypass HIPAA. You need: (1) Patient consent, (2) A valid warrant or court order, OR (3) A specific legal exception like mandatory abuse reporting. 'Active investigation' isn't a magic phrase.

choices.text: I can confirm if they're here, but I need a warrant or patient consent for medical records.
choices.feedback: Correct! Law enforcement requests require proper legal process. You can confirm presence in some circumstances, but medical records require a warrant, court order, or patient authorization. Don't be intimidated by badges.

choices.text: I can't help you at all. Come back with a lawyer.
choices.feedback: Too rigid. HIPAA does allow some law enforcement disclosures with proper legal process. You can work with officers who follow the rules—just don't hand over records without proper authorization.

scenes.dialogue: Please, my mother was just brought in by ambulance. Nobody will tell me anything! Is she okay? What happened? I'm her daughter—I have a right to know!

choices.text: Let me check if your mother has you listed as an authorized contact.
choices.feedback: Correct! Even for worried family members, you need to verify authorization. The patient may have restricted information sharing for personal reasons. Check the authorization list, then share appropriately.

choices.text: She's stable. The doctors are running tests now.
choices.feedback: Wrong. You just assumed authorization without checking. What if the patient is fleeing domestic abuse and doesn't want family to know where she is? Always verify before sharing.

choices.text: I can't tell you anything. HIPAA prevents all family communication.
choices.feedback: Too rigid and incorrect. HIPAA allows sharing with family members involved in care—but you need to verify authorization first. Don't use HIPAA as an excuse to be unhelpful.

scenes.dialogue: Hey, new person! Welcome to the lab. Quick question—do you know what makes something PHI versus just regular health information?

choices.text: PHI is any health information that can be connected to a specific person.
choices.feedback: Correct! PHI = Health Information + Identifiers. The magic formula. If you can connect health data to a specific person (through any of the 18 identifiers), it's PHI and protected under HIPAA.

choices.text: PHI is only diagnoses and test results—nothing else.
choices.feedback: Way too narrow! PHI includes ANY health information tied to identifiable individuals: appointments, billing records, prescription history, even the fact that someone is a patient. It's broader than you think.

choices.text: Everything in a hospital is automatically PHI.
choices.feedback: Close but not quite. De-identified data isn't PHI. If you properly remove all 18 identifiers, it's no longer protected under HIPAA. That's how research data often works.

scenes.dialogue: I'm conducting a study on treatment outcomes. I just need to pull some patient data from the last year. I'm faculty here, so I should have access, right?

choices.text: Faculty status doesn't grant research access. You need IRB approval or patient authorization.
choices.feedback: Correct! Research use of PHI requires either: (1) Patient authorization, (2) IRB waiver, or (3) Use of properly de-identified data. Being faculty doesn't bypass HIPAA. The rules apply to everyone.

choices.text: Sure, you're faculty. Let me show you how to access the database.
choices.feedback: Wrong! Faculty status doesn't override HIPAA. Research access requires proper authorization through IRB or patient consent. You just enabled a potential HIPAA violation.

choices.text: I'll need to check with my supervisor first.
choices.feedback: Good instinct to escalate, but you should know the answer: research requires IRB approval or patient authorization. Escalating is fine, but understand the underlying rule.

scenes.dialogue: I'm here to pick up samples for the reference lab. Can you also give me a printout of the patient info so they know whose samples these are?

choices.text: The sample labels should have the minimum necessary information. I can't give you additional patient details.
choices.feedback: Correct! Third-party couriers and labs need only the minimum necessary information to do their job. That's usually on the sample label. Extra printouts with full patient info are a violation waiting to happen.

choices.text: Sure, let me print their full chart summary for you.
choices.feedback: Wrong! Massive oversharing. Couriers and reference labs need only what's on the sample label. Full chart summaries violate the Minimum Necessary rule and create unnecessary risk.

choices.text: You'll need to request that through proper channels.
choices.feedback: Reasonable response, but be clear about WHY: Minimum Necessary rule. The sample label has what they need. Additional patient info shouldn't be shared with third-party couriers.

scenes.dialogue: You notice sample containers with patient labels sitting on an open counter near the hallway. The labels show full patient names, dates of birth, and the type of test being run (HIV screening, pregnancy test, etc.).

choices.text: This is a HIPAA violation—samples with identifiable PHI shouldn't be left in open areas.
choices.feedback: Correct! Those labels contain PHI (name, DOB, test type). Leaving them visible in open areas violates physical safeguard requirements. Samples should be in secured areas, not on display.

choices.text: This is fine—labs need quick access to samples.
choices.feedback: Wrong. Operational convenience doesn't override privacy. Those labels reveal sensitive health information (HIV tests, pregnancy tests) linked to patient names. This needs to be secured.

scenes.dialogue: Test results are sitting on the counter, clearly visible. You can see patient names, diagnoses, and lab values. The lab tech stepped away about 5 minutes ago.

choices.text: This violates HIPAA—PHI shouldn't be left visible and unattended.
choices.feedback: Correct! Physical safeguards require protecting PHI from unauthorized viewing. Visible results with patient names and diagnoses left unattended is a clear violation. These should be face-down or secured.

choices.text: The tech will be back soon—it's fine.
choices.feedback: Wrong. '5 minutes' is 5 minutes too long for PHI to be visible and unattended. Anyone walking by could see patient names linked to diagnoses. Time doesn't make it okay.

scenes.dialogue: Welcome to Medical Records! This is where patient stories live. Quick quiz: how long does HIPAA give us to respond when a patient requests their own records?

choices.text: 30 days, with one possible 30-day extension if needed.
choices.feedback: Correct! HIPAA gives covered entities 30 days to fulfill access requests, with one optional 30-day extension if you notify the patient and explain why. Don't make them wait longer than necessary though!

choices.text: We can take as long as we need—medical records are complicated.
choices.feedback: Wrong! Patients have a RIGHT to timely access. HIPAA mandates 30 days (plus possible extension). 'It's complicated' isn't an excuse. Delays can result in complaints and penalties.

choices.text: Immediately—patients have instant access rights.
choices.feedback: Close! While we should provide records as quickly as possible, HIPAA allows up to 30 days. Immediate access is ideal when possible, but not legally required.

scenes.dialogue: Hi, I need copies of all my medical records from the past two years. My new doctor wants them, and I'd also like to review everything myself. Can you help?

choices.text: Absolutely. You have the right to access your records. Let me start the request process.
choices.feedback: Correct! Patients have a fundamental right under HIPAA to access their own medical records. Start the request process, explain the timeline (up to 30 days), and offer to send copies directly to their new doctor.

choices.text: You'll need your doctor's permission before I can release anything.
choices.feedback: Wrong! Patients don't need permission from ANYONE to access their own records. This is a core HIPAA right. Don't create barriers that don't exist.

choices.text: I can send them to your new doctor, but you can't have copies for yourself.
choices.feedback: Wrong! Patients have the right to obtain copies for themselves, not just for transfer to other providers. Personal access is explicitly protected under HIPAA.

scenes.dialogue: I have a subpoena for patient records in a malpractice case. I need these records TODAY. My client is depending on this. Don't make me get a judge involved.

choices.text: I'll verify this subpoena is valid and follow our disclosure procedures. This may take a few days.
choices.feedback: Correct! Don't be intimidated. Subpoenas require verification: Is it properly issued? Has the patient been notified? Is there a protective order needed? Follow your facility's legal disclosure procedures, not the attorney's timeline.

choices.text: Here you go—I don't want any legal trouble.
choices.feedback: Wrong! Immediate compliance without verification is a HIPAA violation waiting to happen. Subpoenas need to be verified. The patient may need to be notified. Follow procedure, not pressure.

choices.text: I refuse. Attorneys don't get patient records.
choices.feedback: Too rigid. Valid subpoenas CAN compel disclosure, but they must be verified and proper procedures followed. Don't refuse legitimate legal process—just don't rush it either.

scenes.dialogue: The audit log screen shows recent record access. You notice your coworker accessed a patient's chart 15 times yesterday, but that patient isn't on their assigned floor or care team.

choices.text: This is suspicious and should be reported—accessing records without a job-related need is a violation.
choices.feedback: Correct! Repeated access to records without a treatment, payment, or operations reason is a red flag. This could be curiosity snooping (maybe they know the patient personally?) or worse. Report to your Privacy Officer.

choices.text: They probably have a good reason. Mind your own business.
choices.feedback: Wrong! 'Probably' isn't good enough when it comes to HIPAA. Unusual access patterns need investigation. If you see something, say something. It's literally your job.

choices.text: Ask them directly why they accessed those records.
choices.feedback: Risky approach. They might cover their tracks or get defensive. It's better to report to your Privacy Officer who can investigate properly through audit logs. Don't tip off potential violators.

scenes.dialogue: Welcome to IT Security. Fun fact: most breaches aren't from hackers—they're from employees. What's the #1 thing staff can do to prevent breaches?

choices.text: Use strong, unique passwords and never share login credentials.
choices.feedback: Correct! Password hygiene is breach prevention 101. Shared passwords, weak passwords, and passwords on sticky notes are how breaches happen. Each person needs unique credentials that they never share.

choices.text: Install better antivirus software.
choices.feedback: That's IT's job, not individual staff. The human element—password hygiene, phishing awareness, access control—is where staff make the biggest difference in security.

choices.text: Don't click on suspicious emails.
choices.feedback: Good answer! Phishing is a major threat. But password hygiene is even more fundamental. Both matter, but credential management is the foundation of access control.

scenes.dialogue: Hi, I'm from MedTech Solutions. We maintain your imaging equipment. I need access to the system to run diagnostics. Can you log me in with your credentials real quick?

choices.text: No. Vendors need their own authorized credentials. I can't share mine.
choices.feedback: Correct! Never share your credentials with ANYONE—especially vendors. They should have their own authorized access through your IT department. If they don't, that's their problem to solve properly.

choices.text: Sure, just this once. You're a legitimate vendor.
choices.feedback: Wrong! 'Just this once' is how breaches happen. When you share credentials, every action they take is logged under YOUR name. If something goes wrong, you're responsible. Vendors get their own access or no access.

choices.text: Let me call IT to get you temporary access.
choices.feedback: Good! Involving IT is the right move. They can provide proper vendor credentials with appropriate access levels. Never take shortcuts with access control.

scenes.dialogue: These password requirements are ridiculous! I have to change it every 90 days and remember 47 different systems. Can't you just give me a master password that works everywhere?

choices.text: No. Those requirements exist to protect patient data. Try a password manager.
choices.feedback: Correct! Security requirements aren't arbitrary—they protect patients. Password managers are the solution for 'too many passwords.' Never compromise security for convenience.

choices.text: I'll set up a simpler system for you. Security shouldn't be this hard.
choices.feedback: Wrong! 'Easier' often means 'less secure.' Those password requirements exist because breaches are expensive and harmful. Help them work within the system, not around it.

choices.text: I understand it's frustrating, but these rules protect our patients.
choices.feedback: Good empathy, but also offer a solution: password managers! Don't just validate the frustration—help them comply without going insane.

scenes.dialogue: You're reading the Breach Response Playbook. It outlines what happens when PHI is improperly accessed or disclosed. The first 72 hours are critical.

choices.text: Got it—document everything, notify the Privacy Officer immediately, don't try to fix it alone.
choices.feedback: Correct! Breach response requires: (1) Immediate documentation, (2) Privacy Officer notification, (3) Risk assessment, (4) Possible patient notification, (5) Possible HHS reporting. The cover-up is always worse than the breach.

choices.text: Seems like a lot of paperwork. I'd probably just try to fix it quietly first.
choices.feedback: Wrong! 'Fixing it quietly' is how small mistakes become reportable breaches with massive fines. The process exists for a reason. Follow it. Every time. No exceptions.

scenes.dialogue: *yawning* I've been here 14 hours. I was charting in patient records and I think I might have entered notes in the wrong patient's file. But I'm too tired to check. It's probably fine, right?

choices.text: No, you need to check and correct it now. Wrong-patient documentation is serious.
choices.feedback: Correct! Wrong-patient documentation isn't just a HIPAA issue—it's a patient safety issue. That note could affect treatment decisions. Check it, fix it, and document the correction. Then go home and sleep!

choices.text: Yeah, you're exhausted. Deal with it tomorrow when you're fresh.
choices.feedback: Wrong! Tomorrow might be too late. What if that incorrect note affects treatment decisions tonight? Wrong-patient documentation needs immediate correction. Fatigue doesn't excuse patient safety risks.

choices.text: Just add a note saying you were tired. That covers you legally.
choices.feedback: That's not how documentation works. You can't just annotate 'was tired, might be wrong.' Find the error, fix it properly, and follow your facility's amendment procedures.

scenes.dialogue: You overhear two coworkers at the next table discussing a patient by name, including their diagnosis and family drama. They're speaking at normal volume. Other people can definitely hear.

choices.text: Interrupt politely: 'Hey, we should probably take this somewhere private.'
choices.feedback: Correct! This is a HIPAA violation in progress. Patient names + diagnoses + public space = bad. A gentle redirect protects everyone. Don't be a silent bystander when you can prevent harm.

choices.text: Mind your own business. They're not talking to you.
choices.feedback: Wrong! Witnessing a HIPAA violation and doing nothing makes you complicit. You don't have to be aggressive—just redirect the conversation. Silence enables bad behavior.

choices.text: Report them to HR immediately without saying anything.
choices.feedback: Reporting is appropriate, but why not prevent ongoing harm first? A quick 'let's take this somewhere private' stops the violation immediately. Then report if the behavior continues.

scenes.dialogue: Someone left their work phone unlocked on the table with the patient scheduling app open. You can see today's appointments including patient names and reasons for visit. The owner is nowhere in sight.

choices.text: This is a HIPAA violation—mobile devices with PHI must be secured when unattended.
choices.feedback: Correct! Mobile devices are governed by HIPAA's Security Rule. Unlocked devices with PHI visible are a breach waiting to happen. The owner needs to lock their screen. Consider locking it for them or finding the owner.

choices.text: It's just a phone in the break room. Staff are trustworthy.
choices.feedback: Wrong! 'Trust' doesn't replace security controls. Break rooms aren't secure areas. Anyone could see or photograph that screen. Mobile devices with PHI need the same protection as workstations.
